other_observers: Andrew Spencer and Cole Wild; We found this individual bird; we were split up from the rest of the field trip group at the time.
specific_location: Fox Ranch, about 1 km SE of the headquarters.
description: As the bird flew towards us, and then almost right over our heads at an altitude of about 40 feet, we got a pretty good look at it in binoculars. It was shaped right for a Sprague's Pipit, with a longer tail than a Horned Lark but a shorter one than an American Pipit, and its underparts appeared to be mostly whitish possibly washed with buffy, particularly across the breast, which also may have been lightly streaked. The face definitely seemed blank, without the dark markings of a Horned Lark.Behaviors: The bird was observed only in high prolonged flight; we lost sight of it before it landed.Call: The bird was detected first by its call, a high-pitched, clear, rising, Barn-Swallow-like "squeet!" that was repeated at least 8-10 times while the bird flew towards us from the west, over us, and then off to the south. All renditions of the call were identical, which alone is almost enough to rule out any longspur, Horned Lark, or American Pipit.Plumage: unknown
